transcript:
Here's what's happening: a grid of paper-colored tiles keeps breathing in and out — sometimes clustering into a soft central bloom, sometimes scattering into a dense dark weave, sometimes splitting into hard-edged bands. It never quite settles.

Now look at how each tile decides when to move. In the setup, there's a variable called "fibonacci index," calculated from each tile's distance from the center and scaled by the golden ratio. That number becomes each tile's "phase" — basically its personal timing offset. So tiles don't fold in unison; they fold in rings radiating outward, like a delay line built into the geometry itself before the animation even starts.

Then in the draw loop, watch the gradient — the artist calls it "light reveals dimension." The gradient's midpoint literally moves based on "light intensity," which is just the sine of each tile's rotation. So the shimmer you're seeing isn't a lighting engine, it's color math faking a light source that doesn't exist.

So what looks like folded paper catching real light is actually flat rectangles lying to you convincingly, using nothing but timing offsets and gradient stops.

cues.context: setup, there's a variable called "fibonacci index," calculated from each tile's distance
the draw loop, watch the gradient — the artist calls it
midpoint literally moves based on "light intensity," which is just the sine
nothing but timing offsets and gradient stops.
